Question: The ability of an organism to sense changes that take place within its body is an example of
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Give an example of each level of organization.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Responsiveness (or irritability) is the characteristic of life that enables organisms to sense internal or external environmental changes and react to them.

Question: Which of the following processes is concerned with creating new organisms similar to the parents?
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Describe, in order from simplest to most complex, the major levels of organization in the human organism.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Reproduction is the process of generating new cells or new individual organisms to ensure species continuity.

Question: A section that separates the body into right and left portions would be a
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Identify and define the anatomic planes in which a body might be viewed.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: A sagittal plane divides the body vertically into right and left portions. A midsagittal section divides it into equal right and left halves.

Question: The thoracic cavity lies where in relation to the abdominopelvic cavity?
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Identify and describe the location of the body cavities and the major organs found in each cavity.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: The thoracic cavity is positioned superior to (above) the abdominopelvic cavity, separated by the muscular diaphragm.

Question: Mechanisms to control body temperature and pressure maintaining a stable internal environment are examples of
Subject: B - Homeostasis
SLO Description: Compare and contrast positive and negative feedback in terms of the relationship between stimulus and response, and describe examples of each.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Negative feedback mechanisms act to oppose or reverse the initial deviation from a set point, maintaining homeostatic stability for body temperature and blood pressure.

Question: In homeostasis body parts function efficiently only when the concentrations of water food substances and oxygen and the conditions of heat and pressure
Option A: remain perfectly static and do not vary
Option D: fluctuate greatly between very high and low values
Subject: B - Homeostasis
SLO Description: Define the law of mass balance and relate it to body homeostasis.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Homeostasis maintains dynamic equilibrium where internal physiological variables fluctuate within narrow, compatible limits (normal range).

Question: In the anatomical position what is the position of the palms and forearms?
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Describe the human body in anatomical position.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: In standard anatomical position, the body is standing erect, facing forward, with upper limbs at the sides and palms facing forward (anteriorly), which means the forearms are supinated.

Question: Frontal (or coronal) refers to a cut that divides the body into
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Describe how to use the terms right and left in anatomical reference.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: A frontal (coronal) section divides the body vertically into anterior (front) and posterior (back) portions.

Question: Homeostasis is defined as the
Option A: ability of human beings to keep weight within normal limits
Option B: maintenance of a constant external temperature inside a room
Option C: ingestion of enough food to keep hunger pains from developing
Option D: tendency of the body to maintain a stable environment
Correct Answer: tendency of the body to maintain a stable environment
Subject: B - Homeostasis
SLO Description: List the main physiological variables for which the body attempts to maintain homeostasis.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Homeostasis is the physiological maintenance of a relatively stable internal environment despite continuous external fluctuations.

Question: Which gland is located in the pelvic region?
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: List and describe the location of the major anatomical regions of the body.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: The ovaries are female reproductive glands located in the pelvic cavity of the lower abdominopelvic basin.

Question: Which of the following best describes the fundamental relationship between human anatomy and physiology?
Option A: Anatomy focuses on microscopic chemical reactions while physiology studies outer body shapes.
Option B: Anatomical structure determines physiological function at every level of organization.
Option C: Physiology functions independently of physical anatomical structures.
Option D: Anatomy is only studied in diseased organs, whereas physiology studies healthy organs.
Correct Answer: Anatomical structure determines physiological function at every level of organization.
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Explain how anatomy and physiology are related.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Anatomy (structure) and physiology (function) are intrinsically linked: the structural arrangement of a cell, tissue, or organ dictates what functions it can perform.

Question: In a homeostatic feedback mechanism, which component detects a deviation in a physiological variable and transmits input signals?
Subject: B - Homeostasis
SLO Description: Identify the components of a feedback loop and describe the function of each.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: A receptor (or sensor) monitors the internal environment and detects stimuli (changes from normal range), sending signal inputs to the control center.

Question: The serous membrane that adheres directly to the outer surface of the lungs is the
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Describe the location and function of serous membranes in the body cavities.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: The visceral pleura directly covers the outer surface of each lung, whereas parietal pleura lines the thoracic cavity wall.

Question: In anatomical directional terminology, the elbow is described as being __________ to the wrist.
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Describe and properly use directional terms in anatomical reference.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: Proximal means closer to the point of attachment or trunk. The elbow is closer to the shoulder attachment than the wrist, making it proximal to the wrist.

Question: Which major organ system provides environmental protection, regulates body temperature, and houses cutaneous sensory receptors?
Subject: A - Body Plan & Organization
SLO Description: Identify the major organ systems and describe their principal functions.
Chapter: Chapter 1: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
Explanation: The integumentary system (skin, hair, nails, and glands) protects underlying tissues, regulates body temperature via sweating and cutaneous blood flow, and contains sensory receptors.
